28.1.label: Question 28.1
28.1.question:
Explain the importance of policyholders’ expectations as far as investment returns are concerned,
    for health and care insurance.

Solution: Policyholders’ expectations are unlikely to be very important in general.                            [½]
For the majority of health insurance products, investment returns play a minor role in the benefits
provided, so policyholders will not normally expect investment gains.                          [½]
Exceptions to this may include unit‐linked or with‐profits health insurance products, eg pre‐
funded long‐term care insurance.                                                                     [½]
However, expectations may not be met if the insurer relies on investment returns to such a
degree that underperformance leads to premium increases on reviewable products.                      [½]
In any case, it is important to ensure that customers are treated fairly.                       [½]
[Maximum 2]

28.2.label: Question 28.2
28.2.question:
Describe how an insurer’s investment strategy may be affected by the regulatory framework in
    which it operates.

Solution: There may be restrictions on the types of assets that insurers are allowed to invest in. For
example, overseas investment may not be allowed.                                                     [½]
A weaker restriction would be to restrict the amount of each type of asset (or a particular holding)
an insurer is allowed to hold.                                                                   [½]
There may be a requirement to hold certain minimum proportions in some asset classes,
eg government securities.                                                                            [½]
An insurer may be allowed to invest in whatever it wants, but then be restricted as to what can be
used to demonstrate statutory solvency.                                                        [½]
There may be restrictions on the maximum exposures that an insurer is permitted to have to a
single counterparty or country.                                                              [½]
There may be a requirement to match the liabilities as closely as possible, ...                      [½]
... eg by currency.                                                                                  [½]
Alternatively, mismatching may be allowed, but a reserve required to cover the extra risk
introduced.                                                                                          [½]
There may be a requirement for the value of assets to exceed the value of liabilities by at least a
specified amount (ie there may be a solvency capital requirement).                                [½]
The requirement to maintain this minimum level of capital at all times may restrict the investment
policy by limiting the proportion of assets the insurer feels comfortable investing in more risky
equity‐type assets.                                                                               [½]
The asset mix may also be affected if the valuation rates of interest are determined with
reference to the assets backing the liabilities, eg on long‐term business.                           [½]
There may be a requirement to appoint a custodian to look after certain assets.                 [½]
[Maximum 5]

28.3.label: Question 28.3
28.3.scenario:
Discuss the suitability of the assets to match the following liabilities for a health insurance
    company with substantial free assets:

(a).question:
Liabilities:        the benefits from a block of income protection insurance policies
Assets:             a portfolio of medium‐dated and long‐dated fixed‐interest securities

Income protection policies: For claims currently in payment, the duration of the assets will be too long, so there is a liquidity
risk if it is necessary to sell assets early to meet claims.                                        [½]
For healthy policyholders the assets may be a more suitable match.                                      [½]
However there may still be problems, for:          the outstanding policy durations may still be short relative to the assets, eg if most of the
         the policy benefit may be index‐linked, rather than fixed in monetary terms, which would
leave the insurance company with a risk from high inflation.                         [½]

(b).question:
Liabilities:        a block of conventional active (ie non‐claiming) long‐term care insurance
policies
Assets:             a portfolio of equity shares

Conventional long\u2010term care insuran: The assets will provide an income that is fairly stable and increases in real terms, but the market
They should give positive real returns over the long term.                                              [½]
LTCI liabilities would normally be real, with claims usually related, directly or indirectly (eg by
The liabilities should be reasonably long‐term (as claims are not yet in payment and the policies
must therefore be pre‐funded), so short‐term volatility is less of an issue (ie while the claims are
The assets could therefore provide a reasonable long‐term match.                                        [½]
There could still be a problem in the future when claims come to be paid, as the assets ultimately
need to be sold to meet benefit payments.                                                       [½]
The high volatility of the equity market values could lead to substantial losses.                       [½]
The existence of large free assets could protect the insurance company from insolvency, but the
risk of loss is still significant.                                                            [½]
It is more likely that the insurance company would switch from equities to, say, index‐linked
bonds, as the liabilities become more mature, to reduce the severity of this risk.                      [½]

(c).question:
Liabilities:        the future expenses arising from a block of single premium unit‐linked
long‐term care insurance contracts
Assets:             the future management charges under the contracts. These charges are
at a fixed rate of 1% per annum of the linked funds. (There are no other
expense charges.)

Expenses from linked contracts: Assuming the unit‐fund assets are real assets such as equities or index‐linked bonds, these assets
would be expected to increase in value in real terms in the long term, ...                      [½]
... so this should be a good match with the inflation of expenses in the long term.                     [½]
However, the income is not necessarily a good match in the short term because:                         [½]
         the high volatility of equity market values results in a poor match with inflation‐linked
... cash would be a much better match to short‐term inflation than equities (or long‐dated
         the income is related directly to the market value of the assets, ...                        [½]
... so a short‐term fall in the value of the assets would have a big impact upon the level of
the charges being taken from the unit‐linked fund.                                         [½]
Also, the insurance company’s expenses might not increase in line with the growth in the
economy in which it is operating.                                                                      [½]
However, the insurance company does have significant free assets, so it may be comfortable with
the mismatch, especially in relation to the short term.                                      [½]

28.4.label: Question 28.4
28.4.scenario: A long‐term health insurer transacts all types of without‐profits, health insurance business.
28.4.question: Discuss the extent to which assets and liabilities should be matched.

Solution: Matching means choosing assets that will have an equal and opposite cashflow to that of the
liabilities concerned.                                                                      [½]
In practice, this is often impossible, so the term matching is often used in a looser sense to mean
choosing assets that share the same nature, term and currency as the liabilities being matched as
far as possible.                                                                                  [½]
Matching reduces the exposure to market movements, eg small interest rate or equity market
value movements, ...                                                                       [½]
... however, it removes the possibility of profit as well as loss.                                     [½]
If the free assets are low, the insurer may decide it can write only business that can be matched in
order to protect solvency.                                                                       [½]
If the free assets are larger, the insurer may decide to mismatch in the hope of making a profit if
interest rates or equity market values move as expected.                                          [½]
Extensive matching is unlikely to be achievable on health insurance products because of the
uncertainty of benefit outgo on protection products.                                                   [½]
For example, the incidence and timing of critical illness claims is unknown, so exact matching by
term is not possible.                                                                           [½]
However, where it is achievable, by minimising investment risk, contracts can be competitively
priced using close to current yields as the pricing interest assumption.                       [½]
Exact matching may be impossible for an expanding fund because there are no assets available
with initially negative income and/or that are sufficiently long‐term.                       [½]
A lesser protection is immunisation. Even then, liabilities may be so long that there are no
suitable assets.                                                                               [½]
[Maximum 4]

28.5.label: Question 28.5
28.5.scenario:
’Investment performance is of paramount importance to an insurer writing unit‐linked health and
    care insurance.’
28.5.question: Discuss, with reasons, whether or not you agree with the above comment.

Solution: The sentence is only correct in certain instances.                                                       [½]
Unit\u2010linked products where the bene: For products where the benefit depends on the value of the underlying investments, eg pre‐
funded LTCI, then it is correct to say that investment performance is important.                         [½]
Here, a large part of the investment risk to the insurer is being passed to the policyholder, so to
that extent the investment performance is not critical.                                            [½]
However, it may still be important, as poor returns to policyholders may encourage lapses …              [½]
… and/or reduce the product’s marketability and hence discourage new business.                           [½]
The insurer will still have fixed expenses, and these may not be covered if volumes of in‐force
It may also find that expense charges, which could be dependent on fund value, are reduced in
Unit\u2010linked protection products: Protection policies (eg CI), however, normally pay a guaranteed benefit (so that protection is full
from the start of the policy).                                                                   [½]
For these contracts, the benefit will be paid for by regular risk charges deducted from the unit
Unit funds do not need to be kept at very high levels in order to support these charges, and hence
In these cases, investment performance will not be important.                                            [½]
However, if the insurance company has significant assets that are not linked to unit liabilities
(eg free assets), then their presence could increase the importance of investment returns.       [½]

28.6.label: Question 28.6
28.6.question:
Describe briefly how stochastic cashflow modelling might be used to determine strategic
    investment guidelines for health insurance company investment managers.

For stochastic asset / liability modelli:          specify a solvency measure                                                                     [½]
         specify a profitability measure                                                                [½]
         specify an acceptable probability of ruin                                                      [½]
         model the future behaviour of assets, ...                                                      [½]
... including investment return assumptions                                                    [½]
         model the behaviour of liabilities, ...                                                        [½]
... including deciding on any links between asset behaviour and liability valuation (ie it
must be dynamic); ....                                                                     [½]
... this could include dynamic strategic responses, eg altering the investment strategy
mid‐projection if solvency becomes threatened                                           [½]
         include the effect of consistent expense inflation                                          [½]
         decide upon an allowance for new business                                                   [½]
         decide on a period of projection and calculation frequency                                  [½]
         investigate different investment strategies, ...                                            [½]
... by looking at the effect on ruin probability and profitability                          [½]
         interpret the results.                                                                      [½]
The basic aim is, given actual free assets and a certain acceptable probability of insolvency, to find
the most profitable investment strategy in relation to the insurance company’s liabilities that
leaves the insurance company with not more than its acceptable level of risk.                       [1]
[Maximum 6]

28.7.label: Question 28.7
28.7.scenario:
A small health insurance company sells only critical illness policies, and has done so for many

years. The finance director has suggested that given the recent drop in the market values of the
company’s equity holding, these assets should be moved immediately into direct property.
28.7.question: Discuss the suitability of the director’s proposal.

Solution: To a certain extent, the decision is not a particularly important one.                                [½]
This is because for critical illness policies, reserves are small, ...                                [½]
... and so the investment returns made on the few funds available for investment will not be
crucial to the financial health of the insurer.                                                       [½]
The importance of the decision will depend on the proportion of the total assets that is held in
equities.                                                                                        [½]
However, to the extent that there are funds available for investment, it will still be the general
aim to maximise returns, subject to an acceptable level of risk.                                   [½]
The insurance company should first question the current holding of equities, which are long‐term,
volatile and real in nature.                                                                   [½]
These are not suitable assets for short‐term cashflow concerns, which is likely to be the main
concern for a small insurance company writing critical illness policies.                       [½]
However, they may be a suitable match for some expenses and free assets.                              [½]
Normally a small insurance company, selling only critical illness insurance would be expected to
be very aware of its day‐to‐day cashflow situation, ...                                         [½]
... and hence invested in readily‐realised short‐term assets such as cash and short‐dated
bonds.                                                                                                [½]
If solvency has been threatened by the fall in market values, moving into property with the sale
proceeds would be a bad idea.                                                                   [½]
However, it may be that the insurance company is very solvent, in which case equities might have
been a deliberate mismatch in the hope of higher returns.                                     [½]
To this extent, property should also provide long‐term real returns.                                  [½]
However, direct property is unlikely to:          is dealt with in large units                                                                  [¼]
For a small insurance company this is not practical.                                                    [½]
Indirect property would be a more suitable investment if property exposure is desired.                  [½]
In any case, moving out of equities may also not be a good idea. The recent drop may have
followed a previous gain, in which case the blip can be ignored.                                        [½]
Transferring all in one go is not likely to be a good idea either.                                      [½]
The drop may not be sustained and a rise could follow, which the insurance company would not
If the move out of equities is made, any losses on equities will be crystallised, preventing any
However, if the transfer was made into property, it is possible that further losses could be made if
There may also be tax implications of such a move, which should be considered.                          [½]
The insurance company should also check any regulations on whether the move is allowable. [½]

28.8.label: Question 28.8
28.8.scenario:
A small, but rapidly expanding, health insurance company writes only the following types of

business:
         pre‐funded long‐term care insurance
         group income protection.

It currently has a reasonably high level of free assets.
28.8.question:
Describe the considerations that should be made when setting the current investment strategy
for this company. You need not give details of any modelling that may be carried out.

The company should select investments:          that are appropriate to the nature, term and currency of the liabilities                      [½]
         that maximise the overall return on the assets, including both income and gains.              [½]
The extent to which the company may depart from the first of these in order to meet the second,
depends on the extent of the insurance company’s free assets.                                [½]
Matching \u2013 pre\u2010funded LTCI: For the pre‐funded LTCI business, many policies could be in force for a very long time, with
benefits not being paid out for many years.                                                             [½]
Therefore, long‐term assets are most likely to be appropriate, ...                                      [½]
... although the appropriate term will depend on the mix of the business (eg by age) ...                [½]
... and the expected claim incidence and termination rates.                                             [½]
It is also necessary to consider the nature of the liabilities. For the pre‐funded LTCI business,
benefits could:
         be level or increasing at a known rate (ie guaranteed in money terms), ...                    [½]
... in which case fixed‐interest bonds of an appropriate term might be a suitable match [½]
         be increasing in line with a specified index (ie guaranteed in terms of an index), ...        [½]
... in which case securities that are linked to the same index (if available) might be suitable
         cover the costs of care (ie indemnity‐based benefits), ...                                    [½]
... in which case assets providing a ‘real’ return (such as equities) might be appropriate
         be based on the value of a specified fund of assets or index (as would be the case on a
unit‐linked product), ...                                                               [½]
... in which case investment in the same assets would be appropriate.                         [½]
For benefits that are indemnity‐based, but subject to a cap, a suitable mix of index‐linked
securities and ‘real’ assets should be considered, depending on how likely the cap is to ‘bite’. [½]
Future premiums should also be allowed for. The amount of the premiums will be known at
outset, but may be reviewable.                                                                          [½]
These can be considered as negative benefit payments from a matching point of view.                     [½]
A perfect match will be impossible to achieve in practice. In particular, it will be difficult to find
assets of long enough duration for many policies.                                                      [½]
Matching \u2013 group IP: For the group IP business, contracts will be written over a one (or two) year term, and so it is only
necessary to consider claims for sickness that will arise during this time.                        [½]
The insurer should also consider the expected length of the claim payments.                             [½]
Most claimants are likely to recover within a short time (eg a year).                                   [½]
For these cases, investment returns will not be very significant, and a mixture of cash and short‐
term fixed‐interest bonds would be a suitable match.                                             [½]
However, there will be some claims of much longer duration, eg lasting until the claimant’s
The insurer also needs to consider whether these claims will increase in payment, and if so,
whether the increase is fixed or index‐linked.                                                          [½]
So, for these claims of longer duration, longer‐term bonds – either fixed‐interest or index‐linked
(if suitable) – are likely to be appropriate for matching.                                       [½]
Other considerations: The insurance company should check that the liabilities are matched by assets of the same
currency, which is likely to be the domestic currency (unless there is overseas business).             [½]
The expense liability will also need to be considered.                                                 [½]
This tends to increase over time in a similar way to a related index (probably an earnings index
due to wages being a large part of expenses).                                                    [½]
A suitable match would be bonds linked to such an index (if available).                                [½]
If these are not available, other assets that provide a real return might be suitable, such as
equities and property.                                                                                 [½]
The currently high level of free assets generally means that it can afford to depart from the above
matching strategy to a considerable extent, in the hope of higher returns.                       [½]
Therefore, a higher proportion of high‐yielding assets, eg equities / property, may be desired. [½]
However, a high level of free assets may be required to meet the insurance company’s desire to
expand the business (rather than provide investment freedom). This will limit the extent to which
a mismatched position is appropriate.                                                         [½]
It is also necessary to consider the insurance company’s acceptable level of risk of insolvency
(ie ruin probability).                                                                          [½]
Asset‐liability modelling techniques can be used to investigate the financial impact of various
investment strategies, given the level of free assets.                                          [½]
The insurance company will also need to consider any regulatory constraints on its investment
strategy, such as:                                                                            [½]
         any constraints on whether, and to what extent, any particular asset can be held
         the extent to which any particular asset can be taken into account in demonstrating
solvency
         whether there is any direct requirement to invest in certain kinds of assets (eg government
bonds).                                                                [½ each, maximum 1]
In particular, with a considerable amount of mismatching, there may be a requirement to hold
significantly higher reserves, reducing the available free assets.                           [½]
It may be necessary to consider the extent of diversification required to control specific risk.       [½]
The insurance company should consider the need to hold enough liquid and marketable assets to
meet unexpected payments, ...                                                              [½]
... or to enable advantage to be taken of investment or other opportunities as they arise.             [½]
For an expanding business this is not so much of an issue, because outgo should be exceeded by
premium income.                                                                              [½]
The deferred period on the products should also give the insurer notice of when assets will need
to be realised to pay claims.                                                                  [½]
The insurance company needs to take account of the shareholders’ appetite for risk.                       [½]
Any differences in tax‐efficiency between different investments should be taken into account, to
the extent that this affects the returns obtained.                                             [½]
The insurance company is likely to want its investment strategy to be in line with those of its
competitors.                                                                                              [½]
Any publicly‐stated intentions of investment strategy may need to be honoured.                       [½]
[Maximum 16]

28.9.label: Question 28.9
28.9.scenario:
A health and care insurer sells immediate needs annuities that increase in line with an index of
   prices. It believes that the best matching assets are index‐linked bonds with a range of terms up
to six years.

(i).question:
Outline the factors that affect whether the insurer would mismatch its assets and
liabilities.

Solution: improving the systems and data recording processes
(j)       revising policy design
(k)       improving wording of policy contracts.

(ii).question:
Describe what is meant by an ‘illiquidity premium’ and discuss whether it is appropriate to
allow for an illiquidity premium in the discount rate to value the liabilities.

Difference of using a realistic basis: If using a realistic basis, the initial reserves would be much lower, perhaps even negative. The
reserves should reflect the future profits expected from the business, which should more than
offset the initial costs involved (otherwise there is something wrong with the pricing basis). The
insurer should not therefore get any new business strain.
However, the need to set aside solvency capital could still create new business strain if the capital
required exceeded the expected future profits.
4.4 Valuation data and process check: If the actuary derives the analysis of surplus components from scratch (rather than working
backwards from the valuation result), and they total to give the surplus arising then the actuary
has performed a valuable check on the valuation process. Even if the analysis is not done with
completely independent data, it can still highlight errors, and so it has a useful checking function.
4.5 Other uses of an analysis of surpl: Just as the mortality and withdrawal investigations described earlier would normally be done over
a period of several years, so too the valuation surplus investigations will normally be most useful
when several consecutive years’ investigations are analysed together.
This will allow the actuary to see any significant trends that are emerging, for example a gradual
increase in withdrawal surplus. It will also assist in identifying random fluctuations, which should
help to establish that any observed differences are due to parameter error, and hence more likely
to be worthy of remedial attention.
4.6 Analysis of embedded value profit: A company writing long-term insurance business may also analyse the change over a year
in its embedded value (the sum of net assets and the present value of the expected future
profit from existing business) in order to:
\uf0b7 provide data for use in e:          provide detailed information for publication in the company’s accounts or those of
any parent company, in particular the value of new business taken on by the
Validation of calculations, assumptions: The key here is the validation of assumptions. It is common to use the same assumptions for
embedded value work as for pricing (other than the risk discount rate which is often set higher for
pricing to take account of the extra risks involved in acquiring business). It is crucial that the
assumptions used in product pricing be valid, and their continuing validity can be monitored by
the analysis of embedded value movement.
Reconciliation of successive years\u2019: Due to the complexity of the models used to determine embedded values, and all of the other
opportunities for error to creep in (for example, model points that are not sufficiently
representative), it is essential to check the results thoroughly.
One of the best ways of doing this is to reconcile the embedded value with the previous result,
ie to identify every contribution to the movement in embedded value. So this is really a very
detailed check, which will in turn validate the calculations and data.
The details of how the surplus arising and the change in embedded value can be analysed
will not be examined in Subject SP1, although there is further coverage in Subject SA1.
\uf0b7 the amount of any expense:          the amount of any morbidity (or other transition) profit or loss
\uf0b7 the amount of any withdra:          the impact of free assets on embedded value growth (ie whether spare capital is being
used efficiently, or whether it is just sitting around earning market yields and hence
reducing the overall return achieved by the insurance company?)
         the impact of supervisory solvency capital requirements on the rate of return achieved.
An analysis of surplus looks at the impact of past experience being different to expected, and
hence the impact on the current year’s profits. An analysis of embedded value profits also
includes the impact on future profits, so this type of analysis provides new information (as
compared to an analysis of surplus).
Data for use in executive remuneration s: Executive remuneration schemes should ideally be based on measures that reflect how well an
insurance company is really doing and so reward good management actions. The change in EV
over a year is a good measure to use because it will be increased if, for example, the insurance
company has sold lots of new policies on terms that are expected to be profitable. The change in
supervisory surplus might be a less good measure to use as supervisory surplus may be depressed
Information for accounts: In some countries, it has become common practice for proprietary insurance companies to
publish the embedded value in their accounts.
Published accounts normally misrepresent the real financial situation of an insurance company
because they do not take any credit for the future profits expected to arise from business already
written. This is a particular problem if an insurance company is expanding quickly, because it may
be making large losses and will seem to be in a bad way, even if in reality it is writing a lot of good
business, which will generate large profits in the future.
Accounts also ignore the potential of an insurance company to sell profitable business in the
future, for example due to a new product launch or reorganisation of its salesforce.
Reporting embedded values will show a truer picture, although it is also much less prudent
because it is entirely based on the idea of taking credit for future profits.
Where this is so, insurance companies may also want to highlight the changes in embedded value
from one year to the next. This information will allow shareholders and financial analysts to see
what the real drivers of the insurance company’s profitability are.
5 Using the results: The results of analysing the experience, the surplus arising and the change in the
embedded value will be used by actuaries to reassess their view of the company’s future.
This may result in changes to the assumptions or models used for pricing or reserving, or
changes to the ways in which the business and its risks are managed.
This is describing the feedback aspect of the actuarial control cycle.
For example: (a)       improving the pricing basis
For example, a deterioration in morbidity experience generally could be mitigated by
raising premium rates in the future (for new business only, unless premiums on existing
policies are reviewable).
(b)       establishing / revising the reserving basis
The reserving basis will normally be based loosely on past experience, subject to other
constraints such as regulations. However, regular changes to the reserving basis may
make year‐on‐year comparisons of reserving strength difficult to quantify.
(c)       changing / improving the marketing message
For example, an analysis that shows poor new business volumes in one particular product
may indicate that the advertising spend should be reviewed or that the product should be
marketed in a different way.
(d)       revising sales procedures in terms of training and selection of distributors, wording
and format of literature and the mechanics of any commission payments and
clawback
Monitoring cashflows may indicate, for example, that one particular broker has a poor
administrative process resulting in lengthy delays in premium receipt. A tightening of the
premium collection procedures might help in this case.
(e)       providing for the adequacy of staffing (numbers and competence)
For example, a rapid increase in new business may increase the number of staff required
in a particular area or for a particular product.
(f)       revising underwriting processes
For example, the level of underwriting may need to increase if morbidity rates are high for
products with a high benefit amount.
(g)       revising claims handling processes
(h)       altering the capital allocation methodology
For example, monitoring persistency in one particular product might shows poor results
because it is over‐priced, and thus uncompetitive. It could be over‐priced because of a
high profit loading, or the need to give a high return on the capital allocated to that
particular line of business. A lower capital allocation (if justified) would result in a lower
profit loading, and consequently more competitive prices.
